title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding and modeling the interaction between vehicle yielding (VY) and pedestrian gap acceptance (PGA) behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, and analyzing their effects on traffic flow.
method_summary: A microscopic traffic flow model was developed by integrating probabilistic models of vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance into a car-following model (Full Velocity Difference model). The model uses time-step simulation in MATLAB to describe the interaction between vehicles and pedestrians at unsignalized midblock crosswalks. Validation was performed using empirical data from two crosswalks in Shanghai.
method_family: Microscopic traffic flow model, Car-following model, Simulation-based analysis
datasets: Empirical data from 295 Zhenhua Rd. crosswalk in Shanghai (313 veh/h/ln, 251 ped/h)
Empirical data from 1565 Lingshi Rd. crosswalk in Shanghai (406 veh/h/ln, 172 ped/h)
VY and PGA models developed from data collected at 13 midblock crosswalk locations in Shanghai
tasks: Traffic flow analysis at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
Sensitivity analysis of traffic and geometric factors
Recommendations for control mode selection (unsignalized vs signalized)
metrics: Relative error between model and survey results
Wilcoxon signed-rank test for model validation
baselines: Traditional Full Velocity Difference (FVD) car-following model
Separate VY model from Malenje et al. (2019)
Separate PGA model from Zhao et al. (2019)

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed model accurately describes the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, with maximum relative error less than 10% compared to empirical data
Pedestrian waiting time increases with the number of traffic lanes and vehicular volume but decreases with pedestrian volume, while vehicular delay increases with all three factors
Signal control is recommended when vehicular volume exceeds 480 veh/h/ln or pedestrian volume exceeds 500 ped/h based on 20s vehicular delay threshold (LOS B)
The model converges probabilistic VY and PGA decision models into the car-following framework, enabling analysis of traffic flow impacts from vehicle-pedestrian interactions

limitations.text: The study only considers legal pedestrian crossing behavior at designated crosswalks; illegal pedestrian crossing is not modeled
The model assumes simplified vehicle and pedestrian arrival distributions (Poisson) without considering effects from adjacent intersections
The accuracy depends on the separate VY and PGA behavioral models; combined model accuracy was not guaranteed

future_work: Study illegal pedestrian crossing behavior at locations without designated crosswalks (common in developing countries)
Investigate combined effects of midblock crosswalks and adjacent intersections on traffic flow
Develop adaptive signal control systems that respond to real-time pedestrian and vehicle demand fluctuations
notes: The study uses a logit-based framework for both vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance decisions. The VY model considers gap size, number of lanes, number of waiting pedestrians, median presence, and e-police presence. The PGA model considers gap size, crossing distance, waiting time, and pedestrian position. Key finding: unsignalized crosswalks with more than 4 lanes should be signalized or include central islands.
